title: The tragedy of "Westalgia" – European Council on Foreign Relations
gist: ECFR director Mark Leonard argues Europe's relief after the Ankara NATO summit reflects a self-defeating 'Westalgia' — an elite-driven refusal to accept that US retrenchment is structural and will continue even under a future Democratic administration — that traps Europe in free-riding, under-investment, and subservience, drawing an explicit analogy to post-unification eastern German 'Ostalgie'.

themes.theme: The Ankara summit as an absurd exercise

insights.claim: At the Ankara NATO summit, Trump reiterated his desire to seize Greenland and briefly ordered a halt to all trade with Spain, yet the summit went off without major catastrophe because Trump did not announce troop withdrawals from Europe or moves to leave the alliance, and even pledged to let Ukraine manufacture Patriot air-defence missiles domestically.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: A domestic Ukrainian Patriot-missile manufacturing license is a concrete near-term defence-industrial development worth tracking.
insights.locator: Despite a few unseemly moments in which Trump reiterated his desire to seize Greenland

insights.claim: NATO leaders pledged to raise defence-related spending to 5% of GDP, but the author calls this figure arbitrary and meaningless absent deeper discussion of actual capabilities, noting it was 'pulled out of thin air' the previous year specifically to appease Trump.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: The 5%-of-GDP defence spending target should be read as political theatre rather than a rigorous capability plan — discount headline spending increases that aren't tied to capability gaps.
insights.locator: NATO leaders revelled in their pledges to increase defence-related spending to 5%

insights.claim: European leaders reportedly fell over themselves to praise Trump at the summit, with NATO secretary general Mark Rutte leading what the author calls the 'sycophantic charge' — behaviour the author argues undermines Europe being taken seriously by Trump, Putin, or Xi Jinping.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: Visible European deference to Trump is framed as strategically self-defeating, weakening Europe's negotiating position with all three major powers simultaneously.
insights.locator: European leaders reportedly fell over themselves to praise Trump

insights.claim: The author coins 'Westalgia' — a European nostalgia for the transatlantic relationship's post-Cold War heyday — as directly analogous to Ostalgie, the nostalgia that emerged in eastern Germany after reunification when populations left and West German wealth drove up property prices, and argues Westalgia was on full display at the Ankara summit.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: A durable interpretive framework for reading European political behavior toward the US going forward — useful lens for assessing future summits and policy announcements.
insights.locator: Ironically, Europe's problem today is not just that Ostalgie has held back eastern Germany

insights.claim: Ostalgie has shaped German politics and culture for almost 40 years and is linked by some observers to a refusal in eastern Germany to acknowledge the world has changed, habitual free riding, and instances of parroting Russian talking points on Ukraine.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: Provides the historical precedent underpinning the author's Westalgia thesis — if the pattern holds, Europe's US-dependency mindset could similarly persist for decades.
insights.locator: The term coined to describe this emotion was Ostalgie

insights.claim: The first feature of Westalgia is Europe's refusal to acknowledge the world has moved on: by continuing to act as if US security guarantees remain as ironclad as under Reagan and George H.W. Bush and continuing to buy US weapons, Europe hopes to turn back the clock, but the author argues America has changed fundamentally and US retrenchment will continue even if a Democrat wins the White House in January 2029.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: A high-conviction call that US retrenchment from Europe is structural and bipartisan, not a Trump-specific phenomenon — a key input for long-horizon European defence and foreign-policy planning regardless of the 2028 US election outcome.
insights.locator: Westalgia has several key features. The first is a refusal

insights.claim: Europe's decades of hyper-reliance on American power has had an infantilising effect, causing Europeans to downplay and underestimate their own power and influence even while caught between a predatory Russia, a fickle US and a coercive China.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: Frames Europe's underestimation of its own leverage as a self-inflicted, correctable constraint rather than an objective weakness — implies upside if European self-perception shifts.
insights.locator: Europe's hyper-reliance on American power clearly had an infantilising effect

insights.claim: The second feature of Westalgia is free riding: just as eastern Germans grew accustomed to west-to-east transfer payments (the 'Solidaritätszuschlag'), Europeans have grown accustomed to America bankrolling their security, leading to chronic under-investment not just in defence but also in industrial base, technological sovereignty and energy independence.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: Under-investment extends beyond defence into industrial base, tech sovereignty and energy independence — a broader European competitiveness thesis relevant to sector allocation across those themes.
insights.locator: The second feature of Westalgia is a tendency to free ride

insights.claim: The author argues Europe should stop spending to flatter Trump's vanity and an arbitrary GDP-share target, and instead critically evaluate its actual capability gaps for fighting a high-intensity conflict independently, arguing this approach would make defence spending easier to justify politically.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: A capability-gap-driven approach to defence procurement, rather than headline GDP targets, is the framework the author expects/prefers Europe to shift toward — relevant for how defence budgets get allocated.
insights.locator: Instead of complaining that US citizens no longer want to be responsible

insights.claim: The third feature of Westalgia is subservience to the US president that Europeans do not hide well, which the author says prevents Europe from being taken seriously by Trump, Putin or Xi Jinping, and argues Trump likely respects NATO chief Mark Rutte less than Rutte respects himself.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: Visible deference is portrayed as strategically counterproductive across all three major-power relationships (US, Russia, China) simultaneously.
insights.quote: If there is one man who presumably respects Rutte less than Rutte himself, it is Trump.
insights.locator: Third, in their subservience to the US president

insights.claim: The author distinguishes Ostalgie as a bottom-up phenomenon from Westalgia, which he argues is being driven by elites, implying European political leaders — not publics — are the primary obstacle to adapting to a post-American-guarantee world.
insights.speaker: Mark Leonard
insights.so_what: If elites rather than voters are the binding constraint, political change (leadership turnover) rather than public-opinion shifts is the more relevant catalyst to watch for a European strategic pivot.
insights.quote: Whereas Ostalgie was a bottom-up phenomenon, Westalgia is being driven by elites.
insights.locator: Whereas Ostalgie was a bottom-up phenomenon
